Based on my general knowledge of Linear, this is a great question that highlights a core aspect of the tool's philosophy.

The direct answer is that **Linear does not have a universal, predefined set of issue labels.**

Unlike some tools that come with a default set (e.g., "bug," "enhancement"), Linear provides a blank slate. Labels are completely customizable for each workspace. This allows teams to create a labeling system that is perfectly tailored to their specific workflow, projects, and terminology.

### Common Labels Teams Create in Linear

While there are no built-in labels, most teams converge on similar categories. Here is a list of common labels that teams often create, along with typical color associations:

**By Type of Work:**

*   **Bug** (Red) - An error or unintended behavior in the product.
*   **Feature** (Purple) - A new piece of functionality for the user.
*   **Improvement** (Blue) - An enhancement to an existing feature.
*   **Chore** / **Task** (Gray) - Work that needs to be done but doesn't directly add user-facing value (e.g., refactoring, updating dependencies).
*   **Tech Debt** (Orange) - A task to address a shortcut or suboptimal technical implementation from the past.
*   **Epic** (A distinct, bright color like Teal or Pink) - Often used to group a collection of related issues that form a larger feature.

**By Functional Area or Team:**

*   **Frontend** (Light Blue) - Work related to the user interface.
*   **Backend** (Dark Gray) - Work related to servers, APIs, and databases.
*   **API** (Green) - Specific work on the application programming interface.
*   **Design** (Pink) - Tasks for the design team.
*   **Docs** (Brown) - Work related to writing or updating documentation.
*   **Infra** / **DevOps** (Yellow) - Work related to infrastructure, deployment, and operations.

**By Urgency or Source (though Linear has a separate Priority field for this):**

*   **Security** (Bright Red) - A security vulnerability that needs to be addressed.
*   **Customer-Reported** (Green) - An issue that was reported by a customer, often via an integration like Intercom or Zendesk.
*   **Hotfix** (Bright Orange) - A critical fix that needs to be deployed immediately.

In summary, you and your team have complete freedom to create, name, and color any labels you need within your Linear workspace settings. The list above simply represents common conventions.

### The Five Core Status Types

Linear organizes all issue statuses into five core, unchangeable types that define an issue's stage in the workflow. Every status, whether default or custom, must belong to one of these types.

1.  **Backlog**: Issues that are not yet ready for work.
2.  **Unstarted**: Issues that are planned but not yet started.
3.  **Started**: Issues that are actively being worked on or are in a review state.
4.  **Completed**: Issues that are finished.
5.  **Canceled**: Issues that have been stopped and will not be completed.

---

### Default Statuses by Type

This is the standard set of statuses in a new Linear team, organized by their core type.

#### Backlog
*   **Backlog**: For ideas and tasks that have not yet been prioritized or scheduled for work.

#### Unstarted
*   **Todo**: For planned issues that are ready to be worked on, often in the current or upcoming cycle.

#### Started
*   **In Progress**: For issues that are actively being worked on right now.
*   **In Review**: For issues where the work is complete but is awaiting review (e.g., code review, QA, design feedback).

#### Completed
*   **Done**: For issues that have been successfully completed, reviewed, and accepted.

#### Canceled
*   **Canceled**: For issues that are no longer relevant, will not be worked on, or were created in error.

---

### Special Case: Duplicate Issues

Marking an issue as a "Duplicate" of another will automatically move it to a status of the **Canceled** type (typically the "Canceled" status) and link it to the original issue.

Linear organizes all statuses into five core types, which define the issue's stage in the workflow. While you can create custom status names, each one must belong to one of these five unchangeable types.

### Default Workflow Statuses

This is the standard set of statuses you'll find in a new Linear team.

| Status Name | Type / Category | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Backlog** | `Backlog` | For ideas and tasks that have not yet been prioritized or scheduled for work. |
| **Todo** | `Unstarted` | For planned issues that are ready to be worked on, often in the current or upcoming cycle. |
| **In Progress** | `Started` | For issues that are actively being worked on right now. |
| **In Review** | `Started` | For issues where the work is complete but is awaiting review (e.g., code review, QA, design feedback). |
| **Done** | `Completed` | For issues that have been successfully completed, reviewed, and accepted. |
| **Canceled** | `Canceled` | For issues that are no longer relevant, will not be worked on, or were created in error. |

---

### Special Case: Duplicate

While not a unique status name by default, marking an issue as a "Duplicate" of another will automatically move it to a status of the `Canceled` type (typically the "Canceled" status itself) and link it to the original issue.

| Action | Resulting Status Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mark as Duplicate** | `Canceled` | The issue is closed because it is a duplicate of another existing issue. |

---

### Custom Workflow Statuses

A key feature of Linear is the ability to customize your workflow. You can create new statuses, but each new status **must be assigned to one of the five core types**.

Here are some common examples of custom statuses and the type they would belong to:

| Custom Status Example | Type / Category | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prioritized** | `Unstarted` | An issue in the backlog that has been vetted and is next in line to be moved to `Todo`. |
| **Ready for Dev** | `Unstarted` | An issue where design and product specs are complete and it's ready for engineering to pick up. |
| **Blocked** | `Started` | An issue that was `In Progress` but is now stopped due to an external dependency or obstacle. |
| **In QA** | `Started` | An issue that has been developed and is now with the Quality Assurance team for testing. |
| **Deployed** | `Completed` | An issue that has been completed and shipped to production. |
| **Won't Do** | `Canceled` | A specific reason for canceling an issue, indicating a conscious decision not to pursue it. |

### Summary of Status Types

To recap, every possible status in Linear, whether default or custom, falls into one of these five fundamental types:

1.  **Backlog**: Issues that are not yet ready for work.
2.  **Unstarted**: Issues that are planned but not yet started.
3.  **Started**: Issues that are actively being worked on or are in a review state.
4.  **Completed**: Issues that are finished.
5.  **Canceled**: Issues that have been stopped and will not be completed.

Of course! There are several excellent tools for interacting with Linear, each suited for different purposes.

Let's start with the direct answer to your specific need and then cover the other available tools.

### The Best Tool to List All Possible Issue Statuses

For your specific goal of listing all possible issue statuses, the two best tools are the **Linear GraphQL API** and the **Linear CLI**.

#### 1. Using the Linear GraphQL API (Most Powerful & Flexible)

The API is the definitive source of truth for your workspace's configuration. You can query it to get a structured list of all workflow states (statuses).

**How it works:** You send a GraphQL query to Linear's API endpoint. The query below asks for all `workflowStates`, returning the `id`, `name`, and `type` (e.g., `backlog`, `unstarted`, `started`, `completed`, `canceled`) for each one.

**Example GraphQL Query:**

```graphql
query {
  workflowStates {
    nodes {
      id
      name
      type
    }
  }
}
```

**How to use this:**
*   Go to the **Linear API Playground** in your workspace settings: `Settings > API > Playground`.
*   Paste the query above into the left-hand panel and press the "Play" button.
*   The results will appear on the right, giving you a complete JSON list of all statuses across all your teams.

---

#### 2. Using the Linear CLI (Easiest for Quick Checks)

The official Linear Command Line Interface (CLI) is a fantastic tool for developers who want to interact with Linear from their terminal without writing a full script. It has a dedicated command for this.

**How it works:** Once you have the [Linear CLI installed and configured](https://github.com/linear/cli), you can run a simple command.

**The Command:**

```bash
linear issue:status
```

**Example Output:**
This command will print a clean, human-readable list of all available statuses in your workspace, often grouped by team.

```
✔ Fetched 25 issue statuses

Triage
  - Triage

Engineering
  - Backlog
  - Todo
  - In Progress
  - In Review
  - Done
  - Canceled

Design
  - Needs Scoping
  - In Progress
  - Blocked
  - Done
```

**Conclusion for your specific need:**
*   Use the **Linear CLI** for a quick, easy-to-read list in your terminal.
*   Use the **GraphQL API** if you need this data programmatically for a script or integration.

***

### Comprehensive List of Tools for Interacting with Linear

Here is a broader overview of all the ways you can interact with Linear.

| Tool | Best For | Technical Skill Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Linear GraphQL API** | Building custom applications, complex scripts, and deep integrations. | High (Software Development) |
| **2. Linear SDK (TypeScript)** | Developers using TypeScript/JavaScript to interact with the API more easily. | High (Software Development) |
| **3. Linear CLI** | Quick actions from the terminal, scripting, and developer-centric workflows. | Medium (Comfort with Command Line) |
| **4. Webhooks** | Triggering external actions in real-time when events happen in Linear. | Medium (Handling HTTP requests) |
| **5. Official Integrations** | Connecting Linear seamlessly with other popular tools (GitHub, Slack, Figma, etc.). | Low (Configuration) |
| **6. Automation Platforms** | Creating no-code/low-code workflows connecting Linear to other apps. | Low to Medium |
| **7. The Linear App (UI)** | Day-to-day manual interaction, viewing settings, and managing projects. | Low (End User) |

#### 1. Linear GraphQL API
This is the foundational layer for all programmatic interaction. It's incredibly powerful and allows you to query or mutate (create/update/delete) almost any data in your workspace, including issues, projects, cycles, teams, and workspace settings like statuses.
*   **Docs:** [developers.linear.app](https://developers.linear.app/)

#### 2. Linear SDK
The official Software Development Kit is a TypeScript library that acts as a wrapper around the GraphQL API. It makes it much simpler to write code that interacts with Linear by providing typed methods and objects, so you don't have to write raw GraphQL queries.
*   **Docs:** [developers.linear.app/sdk](https://developers.linear.app/sdk)

#### 3. Linear CLI
A command-line tool for creating issues, managing projects, and querying information directly from your terminal. It's perfect for scripting and for developers who prefer to stay in their terminal.
*   **GitHub Repo:** [github.com/linear/cli](https://github.com/linear/cli)

#### 4. Webhooks
This is for "push" interactions. You can configure Linear to send a real-time notification (an HTTP POST request) to a URL you specify whenever an event occurs (e.g., an issue is created, a comment is added, a status changes). This is essential for building reactive integrations.
*   **Location:** `Settings > API > Webhooks`

#### 5. Official Integrations
Linear provides a rich set of pre-built integrations that connect it to other essential tools in your workflow.
*   **Examples:** GitHub, GitLab, Slack, Figma, Sentry, Zendesk, Front.
*   **Purpose:** Link pull requests to issues, get notifications in Slack, embed Figma designs, etc.

#### 6. Third-Party Automation Platforms (No-Code/Low-Code)
These services allow you to connect Linear to hundreds of other applications without writing code.
*   **Examples:** Zapier, Make (formerly Integromat), Tray.io.
*   **Purpose:** Create a Google Calendar event when a Linear issue is assigned to you, create a Linear issue from a Typeform submission, etc.

#### 7. The Linear App & Desktop Client (The UI)
Finally, the most common tool is the app itself! For your specific task, you can also see all statuses by going to `Settings > Teams > [Select a Team] > Workflow`. This is a manual, non-programmatic way to see the statuses for a specific team.
